topic: rez roads after monsoon — flash flood damage

turns.content: well the monsoons came early this year and they came angry. half the roads out here are washed out
Early monsoons can be brutal, especially on unpaved roads. How bad is the damage where you are? Are you able to get around okay?

depends on your definition of okay. the main road into town is passable but barely. the dirt roads to some of the chapters are gone. not damaged, gone. the water just took them. there is a wash crossing on the road to my auntie's place that is now a 4 foot deep channel

A 4-foot channel where a road used to be -- that's not just damage, that's a completely severed route. Are people in those more remote chapters able to get out for supplies and medical care, or are they effectively cut off?

some are cut off. that is the thing people do not understand about out here. when a road washes out it is not an inconvenience. it means an elder cannot get to their dialysis appointment. it means a family runs out of water because the water truck cannot get through. the rez has two seasons, you know. mud and dust. right now it is mud season

Mud and dust -- that's a stark way to describe it, and painfully accurate. The health implications of cut-off roads are serious, especially for patients on dialysis or other regular treatments. Is there any emergency road repair happening, or does it take a while for crews to get out there?

it takes a while. the Nation has road crews but they are spread thin. 27,000 square miles of reservation and not enough equipment or people. they fix the main highways first which makes sense but the people who live off the dirt roads just have to wait. could be weeks

27,000 square miles with limited crews -- that's an enormous area to cover. Prioritizing main highways is logical but it means the most isolated people wait the longest, which is a tough reality. Have you seen community members doing any improvised road repair themselves?

oh yes. people get their trucks and drag old tires and rocks to fill in the worst spots. my neighbor took his backhoe out yesterday and carved a detour around the washed out crossing near us. nobody asked him to. nobody is paying him. he just did it because people need to get through

That's the kind of community response that keeps things running when the official systems are overwhelmed. Your neighbor with the backhoe is doing real infrastructure work out of pure necessity and generosity. Are the flash floods still a risk right now, or has the immediate storm system passed?

more rain coming this week they say. which means anything that got patched could wash out again. the ground is already saturated so the water just runs off instead of soaking in. every arroyo is a river right now. I watched one go from dry to roaring in about 20 minutes yesterday. that is how fast it happens

Twenty minutes from dry to roaring -- that's terrifying and a real danger for anyone who doesn't know to stay out of arroyos during monsoon season. Flash floods are one of the most underestimated natural hazards. Are people generally knowledgeable about staying clear, or do you see risky crossings?

people who grew up here know. you do not cross a running wash, period. but we get visitors sometimes, tourists heading to Canyon de Chelly, and they think their rental car can handle it. every year someone gets stuck. every year. the fire department has to pull them out and they look surprised like the desert is not supposed to have water

The surprise on their faces must be something. People don't associate deserts with flooding, but flash floods in the Southwest are no joke -- more people die from flash floods than from any other weather event in the desert. Do the roads to Canyon de Chelly hold up better since that's a major tourist destination?

the paved ones do. the scenic routes and the back roads, not so much. but honestly right now I am less worried about tourists and more worried about my patients. I have people who need to see me and they cannot get here. and I cannot get to them. that is the part that sits heavy, you know

That's the hardest part -- knowing people need care and being separated from them by washed-out roads. It's a helpless feeling when the barrier isn't knowledge or resources but literally the ground between you and your patients. I hope the rain this week is lighter than predicted and the roads start recovering.
ahéhee. me too. the ground will dry eventually. it always does. then we will be back to dust season and I will be complaining about that instead. that is how it goes out here
Ha, the eternal cycle of mud and dust. At least you face it with humor. Stay safe out there on those roads, and I hope your neighbor's backhoe keeps holding the line until the crews catch up.
